title: Mould and mildew spray
subtitle: A high-strength tea tree and vinegar spray for bathroom mould
excerpt: A targeted mould and mildew spray made from white vinegar, tea tree essential oil, and distilled water. The acetic acid kills mould spores on contact and the tea tree provides a sustained antifungal effect. Leave on the surface for 10-30 minutes before wiping. Not a substitute for physical removal of heavy mould growth.
sourceNotes: Essential Oil Safety, Tisserand and Young (2014), tea tree antifungal properties. UK Health Security Agency, Guidance on household cleaning products for infection prevention and control (2023), acetic acid against mould. NHS Advice on black mould in the home (2024).

recipe.batchNotes: Scales to bottle size. 500 ml is the practical maximum for a standard trigger-spray bottle.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ready immediately. Shake before each use as the essential oil and water separate on standing.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months in an amber spray bottle away from direct sunlight. The acetic acid is self-preserving; the tea tree essential oil scent may fade after 6-12 months but antimicrobial effectiveness remains. Shake before each use; the oil and water separate on standing.

content.text: A mould and mildew spray. White vinegar (5% acetic acid) is effective against a range of mould species including Aspergillus, Penicillium, and Cladosporium at full concentration; this spray dilutes it to 60% for practical use on tiled surfaces without excessive odour. Tea tree essential oil (Melaleuca alternifolia) has well-documented antifungal activity at 0.5-1% concentration; at 2% in this spray it provides a sustained effect on porous grout and silicone sealant after the vinegar has evaporated.

Never mix this spray with bleach or bleach-based cleaners. Mixing acetic acid with bleach (sodium hypochlorite) produces chlorine gas, which is toxic. For heavy or structural mould growth (spreading across walls, inside wall cavities), the NHS advises consulting a professional; surface sprays alone cannot address the underlying cause. Tea tree essential oil is toxic to cats and dogs; ensure pets are out of the room while spraying and the surface is dry before they re-enter.

items.prepNote: 5% acetic acid white vinegar. 60% of the spray. Higher concentration than the all-purpose spray for increased mould-killing efficacy.
distilled or deionised water. 38% of the spray, minus the essential oil volume.
Melaleuca alternifolia tea tree essential oil. 2% of the spray (approximately 50 drops). The antifungal active. Shake the bottle before each use.

content.text: Pour the white vinegar into the amber spray bottle. Add the distilled water. Add the tea tree essential oil. Fit the trigger-spray top and shake for 30 seconds to disperse the oil as fully as possible (it will re-separate on standing; shake before each use). Label with the contents, date, and a note to shake before use.

Shake the bottle. Ventilate the room (open a window). Spray directly onto the mouldy surface until visibly wet. Leave for 10-30 minutes; the longer dwell time, the more effective the treatment on porous grout. Scrub with an old toothbrush for grout lines and a cloth for tiles or sealant. Rinse and dry the surface. Repeat weekly in high-humidity bathrooms to suppress regrowth.

Safe on: ceramic tiles, glass, silicone sealant (will not damage the sealant; will kill surface mould in the top layer). Not safe on: natural stone; the acetic acid etches marble, granite, limestone, and slate; use a dedicated stone-safe antifungal product on these surfaces. Not suitable for painted walls where the acid may lift or discolour the paint over repeated use; test in a hidden area first.

12 months in an amber spray bottle, away from direct sunlight. The vinegar is indefinitely stable; the tea tree essential oil scent fades over 6-12 months but the antifungal activity of tea tree oil remains effective well past the point where the scent is noticeable.
